questions.text: According to the slides, what is the main utility of the `shutdown()` function in socket programming?
answers: It completely closes the socket descriptor on both ends.
It tells the server that the client has sent the whole request by shutting down part of a full-duplex connection.
It terminates the entire server process.
It clears the socket's internal buffer.
questions.explanation: As stated on Slide 39, `shutdown` shuts down part of a full-duplex connection and can be used to tell the server that we have sent the whole request.

questions.text: Under what specific scenario is `getpeername()` described as useful in the presentation?
answers: When a server needs to discover its own autoselected port.
When a client wants to check if a UDP datagram has arrived.
When a server has called a `fork/exec` combination and only the socket descriptor is known.
When converting IPv4 addresses to IPv6 format.
questions.explanation: Slide 39 notes that `getpeername()` is useful if a server has called a fork/exec combination and only the socket is known.

questions.text: In the UDP server code (`inet_dgr_server.c`), why is `getsockname()` called immediately after binding?
answers: To retrieve and print the port number that the operating system autoselected.
To check if the peer client is still connected.
To clear the socket buffer before receiving messages.
To convert the server's IP address to a presentation string.
questions.explanation: The server sets `server.sin_port = htons(0)` to autoselect a port. It then calls `getsockname()` to discover and print the selected port (Slide 46).

questions.text: Which address translation functions are mentioned as also working with IPv6?
answers: `inet_ntoa` and `inet_addr`
`inet_ntop` and `inet_pton`
`gethostbyaddr` and `gethostbyname`
`getsockname` and `getpeername`
questions.explanation: Slide 40 states as a bonus that `inet_ntop` and `inet_pton` also work with IPv6.

questions.text: What analogy is used on the slides to describe the connectionless nature of UDP?
answers: A telephone call
A physical conveyor belt
Postcards
A streaming television signal
questions.explanation: Slide 41 states: "No Connections: Think postcards, not telephone."

questions.text: Why is the `address_len` parameter in `recvfrom()` considered a value-result parameter?
answers: It must be initialized to the size of the destination address structure, and upon return, it contains the actual size of the source address.
It only returns the status of the received packet size without requiring initialization.
It is used exclusively to pass the flags value into the function.
It allows the function to return multiple socket descriptors simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Slide 43 notes that `address_len` is value-result and must be initialized to the size of the buffer pointed to by the address pointer. On return, it holds the actual length of the address written.

questions.text: What potential reliability 'BUG' is highlighted in the simple UDP echo client-server implementation?
answers: The client will crash if the server sends more than 256 bytes.
Since UDP is unreliable, a lost packet will cause the client to hang indefinitely waiting in `recvfrom()`.
The `bind()` call on the client side will fail if a server is already running.
The program will leak file descriptors because `close()` is never called.
questions.explanation: Slide 49 highlights that while everything runs ok, there is a bug because UDP is unreliable. Since `recvfrom()` is blocking, a lost request or response packet will leave the client waiting forever.

questions.text: In `rlsd.c`, what is the benefit of calling `fdopen()` on the active client socket?
answers: It converts the socket from a TCP connection to a UDP connection.
It allows buffered standard I/O (like `fgets()` and `putc()`) to be used directly on the socket as a file stream.
It automatically handles reverse DNS lookups for the client.
It encrypts the communication channel between client and server.
questions.explanation: Slide 51 notes that `fdopen` allows buffered I/O by opening a socket as a file stream.

questions.text: How does the `sanitize()` function in `rlsd.c` mitigate potential security vulnerabilities?
answers: By verifying that the client has administrative privileges.
By encrypting the input string before sending it to `popen()`.
By removing everything from the directory name except slashes and alphanumeric characters, preventing command injection.
By ensuring the directory name does not exceed `BUFSIZ` characters.
questions.explanation: Slide 54 shows `sanitize()` filters the string to only allow slashes (`/`) and alphanumeric characters (`isalnum`), preventing dangerous characters like semicolons (`;`) or wildcards (`*`) from executing arbitrary commands.

questions.text: Why does `rls.c` use a custom `write_all()` function instead of calling `write()` just once?
answers: Because standard `write()` does not support TCP sockets.
To ensure all requested bytes are sent, as `write()` can perform a partial write and return fewer bytes than requested.
To automatically insert a newline character at the end of every message.
To measure and log the latency of the network transfer.
questions.explanation: Slide 55 states that `write_all` guarantees to send all bytes requested, provided no error occurs, by repeatedly calling `write()` in a loop if partial writes happen.

questions.text: In the Rock-Paper-Scissors system, how does the referee communicate with Player 1 (local) and Player 2 (remote)?
answers: Through sockets for Player 1 and pipes for Player 2.
Through shared memory for Player 1 and signals for Player 2.
Through pipes for Player 1 and TCP sockets for Player 2.
Through standard input/output for both players.
questions.explanation: Slide 58 highlights: "Referee talks to the local process through pipes" and "Referee talks to the remote process through sockets."

questions.text: What is the custom message protocol design used in the `write_data()` and `read_data()` functions of the Rock-Paper-Scissors code?
answers: Messages are null-terminated, and the receiver reads until it encounters `\0`.
The first byte of the message contains the length of the string, which the receiver reads first to know how many subsequent characters to read.
Messages are of a fixed size of 60 bytes, and the excess is padded with spaces.
The receiver continuously reads until the socket is closed by the sender.
questions.explanation: As shown in Slide 29 (67/73), `write_data` finds the length (`strlen(message) + 1`) and sends it. `read_data` first reads a single byte representing length, then loops to read that exact number of characters.

questions.text: In the referee scoring function `prs()`, how does the program determine which player won?
answers: By evaluating the alphabetical order of the moves.
By checking the difference between the lengths of the play strings.
By assigning numerical values using an external configuration file.
By checking the socket port numbers of the players.
questions.explanation: Slide 30 (68/73) shows that `prs` calculates the difference in string lengths: `len1 - len2`. Since "PAPER" is 5 letters, "ROCK" is 4, and "SCISSORS" is 8: 5-4=1, 8-5=3, and 4-8=-4 indicate Player 1 wins.

questions.text: Based on the referee's `prs()` logic, how is a draw detected?
answers: When the result of `len1 - len2` is 0.
When the result of `len1 - len2` is -1.
When either string length is less than 3.
When the game times out after 5 seconds.
questions.explanation: Slide 30 (68/73) notes: "If we have a zero, then we call it a draw and nobody get points".

questions.text: What is the key difference between the `getsockname()` and `getpeername()` functions?
answers: `getsockname()` retrieves the address bound to the local socket, while `getpeername()` retrieves the address of the remote peer connected to the socket.
`getsockname()` works only with TCP, while `getpeername()` works only with UDP.
`getsockname()` resolves hostnames, while `getpeername()` resolves IP addresses.
`getsockname()` is used by clients, while `getpeername()` is used only by servers.
questions.explanation: Slide 39 defines `getsockname` as returning the current address to which the socket is bound, and `getpeername` as getting the name (address) of the peer connected to a socket.

questions.text: Which function should be used to convert an IP address string (presentation format) into a network format binary structure?
answers: `inet_ntoa`
`inet_ntop`
`inet_pton`
`inet_addr`
questions.explanation: Slide 40 shows `inet_pton` converts presentation format address to network format (pton = presentation to network).

questions.text: In `inet_dgr_server.c`, what is the effect of setting `server.sin_port = htons(0)`?
answers: It disables port binding and runs the server in local-only mode.
It instructs the operating system to automatically select an available port.
It binds the server to port 80 for HTTP traffic.
It causes the server to fail immediately because port 0 is invalid.
questions.explanation: Slide 46 has a comment pointing to `server.sin_port = htons(0);` which says `/* Autoselect a port */`.

questions.text: In the referee process code, why does the child process (Player 1) close `fd1[WRITE]` and `fd2[READ]` immediately after forking?
answers: To release system memory allocated for buffers.
To prevent the child from writing to parent-to-child pipe `fd1`, and reading from child-to-parent pipe `fd2`.
Because the child does not communicate with the referee process at all.
To force the child process to terminate immediately.
questions.explanation: Pipe `fd1` is used for parent-to-child communication, so the child only reads from it and closes the write end (`fd1[WRITE]`). Pipe `fd2` is for child-to-parent communication, so the child only writes to it and closes the read end (`fd2[READ]`) (Slide 25).

questions.text: In the Rock-Paper-Scissors Client code, how is the user's play choice parsed using a switch statement?
answers: It parses the entire string using `strcmp` in a nested loop.
It evaluates the first character of the input choice ('p', 'r', or 's').
It uses the string length of the choice directly.
It converts the string to uppercase and performs a hash calculation.
questions.explanation: Slide 34 (72/73) shows a switch statement on `*opt` (the first character of the choice), matching `'p'` for paper, `'r'` for rock, and `'s'` for scissors.

questions.text: Why does `rlsd.c` use `popen()` to run the `ls` command instead of `system()`?
answers: `system()` is deprecated in modern C libraries.
`popen()` is faster because it does not create a new process.
`popen()` creates a pipe that allows the program to read the output of the executed command directly as a stream.
`system()` cannot take arguments like a directory path.
questions.explanation: Slide 52 and 53 show `popen` is used with mode `"r"` (`pipe_fp = popen(command, "r")`), which opens a command pipe so the program can transfer the data (command output) directly to the client socket.
